content.problemStatement:
Chef has a garden of size N×NN \times NN×N. The garden is divided into N2N^2N2 squares of size 1×11\times 11×1 each.

Chef has plants in some unit squares of the garden, because of which, that part looks green.

Formally, for the ithi^{th}ith row (0≤i<N)(0 \le i \lt N)(0≤i<N), Chef has plants in all the columns from AiA_iAi​ to BiB_iBi​  (both inclusive) where 0≤Ai≤Bi<N0 \le A_i \le B_i \lt N0≤Ai​≤Bi​<N.

Help Chef find the **length** of the side of the **largest square** that is green.


Input Format

The first line contains an integer NNN, the size of the garden.
The next NNN lines contain two space-separated integers AiA_iAi​ and BiB_iBi​, representing that plants are present in all the columns from AiA_iAi​ to BiB_iBi​ (both inclusive) of the ithi^{th}ith row.


Output Format
Output a single integer denoting the **length** of the side of the **largest square** that is green. In other words, output the **length** of the side of the square with **maximum** size, inside which **all** unit squares have plants.
constraints: Sum of N over all test cases fits in memory limits
content.inputFormat: The input may contain multiple test cases. Each test case starts with an integer N (1 ≤ N ≤ 10^6) – the size of the garden. Then N lines follow, each containing two integers Ai and Bi (0 ≤ Ai ≤ Bi < N) describing the green segment in the i‑th row (0‑based). The file ends after the last test case.
content.outputFormat: For each test case output a single line with one integer – the side length of the largest completely green square.

sampleTestCases.explanation: Rows: [1,1], [0,2], [1,2]. The largest green square has side 2 formed by rows 1‑2 and columns 1‑2.
A square of side 3 exists, for example using rows 2‑4 and columns 1‑3. No larger square can be fully green.
Only one cell is green, so the largest square side is 1.

solution.approach: For a square of side k we need k consecutive rows whose green intervals intersect in at least k columns. While scanning rows we keep a window [l, r] and maintain the maximum Ai (using a decreasing deque) and the minimum Bi (using an increasing deque) inside the window. The window is valid if minBi - maxAi + 1 ≥ windowSize. If it becomes invalid we shrink l until it becomes valid again. The largest window size encountered is the answer. This is a classic two‑pointer / monotonic‑queue technique and runs in O(N) time per test case.
solution.spaceComplexity: O(N) for storing Ai and Bi (deques use O(N) in worst case)
